label: Creating Custom Item Scripts
title: Creating Custom Item Scripts
lead: Attach MonoBehaviours to custom items, read custom item metadata, and structure wearable runtime logic.
bodyHtml:
When to use a custom item script

Use a custom item script when plain useAction, useLimbAction, ToolProperties, or wearable stats are not enough. A script is the right tool when the item needs its own runtime state, periodic logic, event-style reactions, or behavior that depends on whether it is lying in the world, held, or worn.

CUCoreLib adds item scripts through CustomItemInfo.AddSpawnComponent<T>(). That helper stores the correct assembly-qualified type name in SpawnComponents, and CUCoreLib attaches the MonoBehaviour to each spawned item instance the first time the item appears.

If you have not read the broader custom-item surface yet, keep the Advanced Item API page open beside this one. That page covers the item fields and built-in modules; this page is specifically about the script side.

How the script gets attached

Register the item normally, then chain AddSpawnComponent<YourScript>() on the CustomItemInfo object. Your script runs on the spawned item GameObject, so you can use standard Unity callbacks like Awake, Start, and Update.

ItemRegistry.Register(
    "stormmantle",
    new CustomItemInfo
    {
        fullName = "Storm Mantle",
        category = "tool",
        wearable = true,
        wearableCanBeHeld = true,
        desiredWearLimb = "UpTorso",
        wearSlotId = "outertorso"
    }
    .AddSpawnComponent<StormMantleScript>(),
    icon
);

The script is added to every real item instance. That means dropped items, spawned loot, save-loaded items, and equipped wearables all use the same script class.

Reading the item and its custom metadata

Inside the script, cache the attached Item component first. If you need registration-time tuning values, use ItemRegistry.TryGetCustomData<T> or CUCoreUtils.TryGetCustomItemInfo instead of hard-coding everything directly into the script.

private Item item;
private float warmthBonus;

private void Awake()
{
    item = GetComponent<Item>();

    if (item != null && ItemRegistry.TryGetCustomData<float>(item, "warmthBonus", out float configuredWarmth))
        warmthBonus = configuredWarmth;
}

Use CustomData for small registration-time knobs like ranges, multipliers, effect durations, or mode names. Keep mutable per-instance state on the script itself.

Equippable example

This example registers a wearable mantle and adds a script that only applies its effect while the item is actually worn. The script detects the worn state by checking whether the item sits under a Limb transform, which matches the same parentage CUCoreLib already uses for worn-sprite refresh.

ItemRegistry.Register(
    "stormmantle",
    new CustomItemInfo
    {
        fullName = "Storm Mantle",
        description = "A lined mantle that steadies the body in cold weather.",
        category = "tool",
        wearable = true,
        wearableCanBeHeld = true,
        desiredWearLimb = "UpTorso",
        wearSlotId = "outertorso",
        wearableIsolation = 0.16f,
        wearableArmor = 0.08f,
        weight = 1.3f,
        value = 24,
        tags = "cangetwet",
        rec = new Recognition(5),
        CustomData =
        {
            ["warmthBonus"] = 0.35f
        }
    }
    .AddSpawnComponent<StormMantleScript>(),
    AssetLoader.LoadEmbeddedSprite("Images.stormmantle.png")
);

The paired script can then grant a mild warmth effect only while equipped:

public sealed class StormMantleScript : MonoBehaviour
{
    private Item item;
    private float warmthBonus = 0.25f;

    private void Awake()
    {
        item = GetComponent<Item>();

        if (item != null && ItemRegistry.TryGetCustomData<float>(item, "warmthBonus", out float configuredWarmth))
            warmthBonus = configuredWarmth;
    }

    private void Update()
    {
        Limb wornLimb = item != null && item.transform.parent != null
            ? item.transform.parent.GetComponent<Limb>()
            : null;
        if (wornLimb == null) return;

        Body body = wornLimb.body;
        if (body == null) return;

        body.clothingTemperature += warmthBonus * Time.deltaTime;
    }
}

This pattern works well for passive wearable effects: warmth, movement modifiers, periodic healing, visual state, or compatibility hooks into other systems.

Practical guidance

  • Cache Item and any other required components in Awake or Start instead of calling GetComponent every frame.
  • Keep wearable-only behavior behind a worn-state check. The same item script also exists while the item is dropped or sitting in inventory.
  • Use CustomData for tuning constants and the script fields for mutable state.
  • If your script needs save data, use CUCoreLib's save/status systems. A MonoBehaviour field alone is not a save format.
  • Reach for plain useAction or built-in item modules first when they already solve the problem. Scripts are for the cases that actually need runtime behavior.
